title-group.article-title: Linear Mixed Model of Virus Disinfection by Free Chlorine to Harmonize Data Collected across Broad Environmental Conditions

contrib-group.aff: †Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Michigan, 1351 Beal Ave., Ann Arbor, Michigan 48109-2138, United States
‡Department of Internal Medicine, University of Michigan Medical School, NCRC Bldg. 16 #471C, 2800 Plymouth Rd., Ann Arbor, Michigan 48109-2138, United States
△Civil and Environmental Engineering, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina, 27708, United States

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Authors. Published by American Chemical Society

license.license-p: Permits non-commercial access and re-use, provided that author attribution and integrity are maintained; but does not permit creation of adaptations or other derivative works (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Despite the critical importance of virus disinfection by chlorine, our fundamental understanding of the relative susceptibility of different viruses to chlorine and robust quantitative relationships between virus disinfection rate constants and environmental parameters remains limited. We conducted a systematic review of virus inactivation by free chlorine and used the resulting data set to develop a linear mixed model that estimates chlorine inactivation rate constants for viruses based on experimental conditions. 570 data points were collected in our systematic review, representing 82 viruses over a broad range of environmental conditions. The harmonized inactivation rate constants under reference conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 20 °C, [Cl–] < 50 mM) spanned 5 orders of magnitude, ranging from 0.0196 to 1150 L mg–1 min–1, and uncovered important trends between viruses. Whereas common surrogate bacteriophage MS2 does not serve as a conservative chlorine disinfection surrogate for many human viruses, CVB5 was one of the most resistant viruses in the data set. The model quantifies the role of pH, temperature, and chloride levels across viruses, and an online tool allows users to estimate rate constants for viruses and conditions of interest. Results from the model identified potential shortcomings in current U.S. EPA drinking water disinfection requirements.

Viruses must be effectively treated by disinfection processes to protect public health. We harmonize literature data on virus inactivation by chlorine to determine the chlorine doses required to remove viruses and whether current regulations are adequately protective.

institution-wrap.institution: Division of Chemical, Bioengineering, Environmental, and Transport Systems

fn.p: This paper was published ASAP on June 26, 2024, with several typos. The corrected version was reposted on July 9, 2024.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Human and animal viruses play critical roles in human health and well-being. Human noroviruses, for example, cause an estimated 19–21 million annual cases of acute gastroenteritis in the U.S., resulting in tens of thousands of hospitalizations and hundreds of deaths annually1 and an annual economic burden of 10.6 billion dollars.2 Mitigating the spread of viruses is critical to human health and economics, and the disinfection of air,3 surfaces,4 water,5 and food6 is a major route to controlling virus transmission. Free chlorine is the most widely used water disinfectant in the United States and is essential for controlling viruses in drinking water and wastewater treatment, as well as in medical7 and agricultural8 applications. Designing effective chlorine disinfection approaches while also controlling for risks from disinfection byproducts requires accurate disinfection kinetics data for the viruses and conditions of interest.

The kinetics of virus disinfection by chlorine are highly dependent on water quality parameters. Water pH has a large impact on this process, largely because the pKa of hypochlorous acid (HOCl) lies close to the typical pH for water disinfection, and because HOCl is a more effective disinfectant than hypochlorite (OCl–).9 Temperature also affects virus inactivation rates, with rate constants increasing with increasing temperature.9,10 The importance of temperature and pH to virus disinfection by free chlorine is reflected in water disinfection requirements, such as the concentration–time (CT) framework used by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA). The CT framework requires higher CT values for lower temperatures and higher pH values.11 Other water quality attributes, including chloride concentrations12−14 and turbidity15,16 also impact virus inactivation rates with chlorine. The broad range of water types and environmental conditions used in published virus inactivation studies complicates efforts to identify the relative impact of each water quality parameter on virus disinfection and the ways those parameters interact. In their regular review of microbial requirements, the U.S. EPA noted that the variety of viruses, experimental conditions, and analytical methods make intrastudy comparisons difficult.17 An assessment of regulations based on virus inactivation studies would be aided by the harmonization of virus inactivation data.

Under identical environmental conditions, inactivation rate constants differ greatly between viruses.14,18−24 For example, CT values for 3-log10 virus inactivation are over 1000x greater for coxsackievirus B5 (CVB5) than for murine norovirus or human adenovirus 2.24 Order-of magnitude differences in rate constants have been observed even for viruses of the same species.25−27 For example, CT values for 2-log10 inactivation of distinct viruses belonging to the species Enterovirus B varied from 0.82 to 3.6 mg*min/L,27 and some CVB5 environmental isolates have rate constants up to five times lower than the laboratory strain CVB5 Faulkner.25 Mechanistic studies on closely related viruses have identified specific virus characteristics, such as genome and protein composition, that contribute to these differences in kinetics28−30 but it is not known whether those characteristics have universal impacts across viruses. Waterborne viruses have high structural diversity and cause many illnesses with varying severity. A better understanding of the relative susceptibility of different groups of viruses to chlorine would help identify more general virus inactivation mechanisms and help in the selection of surrogate viruses for chlorine disinfection processes.

Models are frequently used to fill in experimental disinfection data gaps, as it is not feasible to test the susceptibility of every virus to free chlorine under every relevant condition. For example, data collected within individual studies have been incorporated into models that predict chlorine inactivation for human adenovirus 2 and CVB5 with water quality parameters as inputs.9,31 More recently, Kadoya et al. used literature data to build predictive models for chlorine disinfection of viruses as a function of pH, temperature, assay, and water type.32 Their models focused on select groupings of human viruses and identified differences within virus species (e.g., different strains of poliovirus), rather than between virus species or families. As the virus disinfection literature encompasses a broad range of viruses and environmental conditions, an opportunity exists to build robust statistical models to identify important trends among both broad virus groupings and environmental conditions.

In this study, we conduct a systematic review to generate a data set of high-quality chlorine disinfection rate constants for a broad range of human and nonhuman viruses. We then use these data to build a linear mixed model to identify major trends in susceptibility between groups of viruses (e.g., poliovirus versus MS2 bacteriophage) and quantify the roles of environmental conditions and study-specific impacts (i.e., random effects) on virus disinfection rates. The model enables the estimation of inactivation rate constants for studied viruses under specific environmental conditions that are not reported in the literature. The resulting data and models are valuable resources for the widespread application of chlorine disinfection, will facilitate the selection of conservative surrogates for viruses of concern, and will identify research gaps that can move us closer to predicting chlorine inactivation of any virus, including emerging and nonculturable viruses, under relevant environmental conditions.

sec.title: Systematic Review
sec.p: We conducted a systematic review on virus inactivation by free chlorine, according to the guidelines published by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA).33 A literature search was conducted with Web of Science and Scopus on January 13, 2021. We set criteria to ensure that chlorine concentrations and virus infectivity were accurately reported and that chlorine concentrations were relevant for water and wastewater treatment systems. Specifically, criteria included: virus removal was assessed using culture-based assays; temperature and pH were reported; chlorine decay was described or the study was conducted with experimental protocols sufficient to minimize chlorine decay; the study was conducted on viruses in solution that had not been artificially aggregated; the study was conducted in pure water, buffers, natural waters or wastewater that had undergone secondary treatment; the reported data included at least one time point under 5 min; the chlorine concentration was less than 20 mg/L and thus within or slightly above the ranges used for drinking water and wastewater treatment;34,35 sufficient kinetic data was reported to extract second-order rate constants; the study was performed on a virus with a publicly available genome. Additional details of the review are provided in Supporting Information 1.

sec.title: Rate Constant Extraction
sec.p: Two reviewers extracted all raw data using Digitizeit software36 and independently calculated second-order rate constants. Rate constants were calculated from raw virus inactivation data whenever possible. Rate constants reported in the study were extracted when raw data were not available. In cases where inactivation exhibited tailing, rate constants were calculated using data from the inactivation phase before tailing was evident. Second-order rate constants were calculated for each data set based on the Chick-Watson model:where t is exposure time of the virus to chlorine, N and N0 are the concentrations of infectious viruses at times t and zero, respectively, k is the second-order rate constant, and Cn is chlorine concentration to the power of the coefficient of dilution n. We assumed n was equal to 1 so that the rate constants could be directly compared. This results in the following equation:which was used to determine the second-order rate constant k with units of L mg–1 min–1, as described in Supporting Information 2.

We assessed agreement between reviewers for each data point using Lin’s concordance correlation coefficient.37 Rate constants presented and used for modeling are the mean of the values calculated by the two reviewers.

sec.title: Variable Development
sec.p: We extracted details on experimental conditions, virus taxonomy, and study authors from the manuscripts and online databases and ultimately tested several of these as terms in our models (Table S2). We refer to data extracted from manuscripts and online databases as “variables”. We refer to the variables, variable derivatives, and interactions that comprise the model feature matrix as “terms”. Experimental condition variables were checked by a second reviewer for all data points.

To be considered in the model, we required variables to be 1) associated with chlorine inactivation rates in the literature and 2) reported in most papers included in the data set. Variables and variable derivatives considered in the model included temperature, pH (as well as ΔpH2 and αHOCl), water type (categorized as buffer solutions, low organics natural or treated waters, high organics natural or treated water, and ultrapure water), chloride content (defined as “high chloride” or “low chloride”, representing chloride concentrations greater than or less than 50 mM, respectively), virus purity, virus classification, virus name, interaction terms (between pH and chloride level, between pH and temperature), and study identifiers, including corresponding author and year of publication. ΔpH2 was defined as the square of the difference between the reported pH value and 7.53, and αHOCl (proportion of the free chlorine as HOCl) was calculated using the equation αHOCl = [H+]/([H+] + Ka). Details of these variables, including assumptions and justifications, are outlined in the Supporting Information 3. We note that bromide concentrations and initial chlorine concentrations had been associated with virus inactivation rates in the literature but were not reported by most papers in our data set.10,38

sec.title: Primary Model
sec.p: We developed a linear mixed model to probe the relative impact of experimental conditions, virus taxonomy and physical properties, and within-paper variation (e.g., systematic dependence among rate constants reported within a certain paper) on virus inactivation by free chlorine. The initial inactivation rate constants were consistent with a log-normal distribution (Figure S3); we therefore fit the log10 inactivation rate constants as the dependent variable in the linear mixed model.

In the primary model analysis, we used a best-subset procedure to select terms based on Akaike Information Criterion (AIC). By evaluating all potential combinations of collinear terms, a best-subset analysis avoids the risk that, due to multicollinearity, the final model depends on the order in which terms are tested. The AIC is a common framework for model parameter selection and estimation that leads to efficient estimates.39 Only terms that improved the log likelihood enough to overcome the penalty imposed by the AIC for each additional parameter were accepted into the model. Conditional R2 values were calculated using MuMin package40 which bases R2 values on equations developed by Nakagawa et al.41,42

We fit models to the following equation:where y is an n-vector of the log10 inactivation rate constants, X is an (n × p) feature matrix of independent variables with n samples and p features, β is a p-vector of model coefficients, Z is a random effect design matrix with binary columns identifying rate constants from the same paper (or, in some models, the same corresponding author), η is an n-vector of Gaussian random effects with mean 0, and ϵ is an independent and identically distributed vector of model errors.

We tested 24 terms in the best-subset analysis, namely pH, ΔpH2, αHOCl, year of publication, virus purification level, high chloride, water type, pH and high chloride interaction, pH and temperature interaction, and interactions between experimental conditions (temperature, pH, ΔpH2) and virus characteristics (Baltimore class or DNA vs RNA genome). The justification for the interaction terms is described below. We included virus name and the per-paper random intercepts a priori because these represent the minimal design to address the research question of estimating virus inactivation rates with free chlorine. Temperature was included a priori due to its widely established role in virus inactivation kinetics9,10,18 and to reduce the computational cost of the best-subset selection.

sec.title: Derivation of Interaction Terms
sec.p: The ΔpH2 term as well as the interaction terms for Baltimore class with pH, ΔpH2 and temperature, and Baltimore class with nucleic acid type were identified through an initial stagewise-forward model selection process (SI 5). The terms of this initial model were virus name, temperature, pH, high chloride, pH and temperature interaction, pH and chloride interaction, and paper identifier as a random effect. Model residuals were broken down by Baltimore class. The residuals plots (estimated vs observed values) of dsDNA for estimated vs observed, dsDNA and pH, dsRNA and pH, and ssDNA and temperature, exhibited trends that suggested additional interactions not captured by the model (Figure S5). We therefore created variables for the interactions between experimental conditions (temperature, pH, ΔpH2) and virus characteristics (Baltimore class or DNA vs RNA genome).

sec.title: Sensitivity Analyses
sec.p: The impact of the collinearity of certain model terms such as pH and αHOCl on the selected model are addressed by the best-subset model selection process. Nonetheless, we conducted a sensitivity analysis by adding terms individually to the primary model and calculating the adjusted generalized standard error inflation factor (aGSIF), which adjusts the generalized variance inflation factor for terms with multiple degrees of freedom. This identifies terms that may have been excluded from the model due to multicollinearity (SI 6.1). To test the impact that using AIC as our information criterion had on the terms included in our primary model, we tested our model selection process with the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). The BIC is a closely related information criterion that selects for simpler models than the AIC by imposing a larger penalty on each additional parameter. Including this analysis provides more confidence in the terms used in the final model. As the taxonomy-related variables in our data set are collinear, a linear mixed model of this data set can include only one taxonomy-related independent variable as a fixed effect. To identify the most appropriate taxonomic variable for our model, we substituted virus name with taxonomic units of species, genus, family, and Baltimore class in our model (SI 6.2). We tested the assumptions of high or low chloride with a maximum entropy sensitivity analysis for cases where chloride level was inferred because it was not directly reported (SI 7).

Analyses were performed in R 4.2.1 using the lme4 package (R, lme4).43 The data files and the scripts for data processing, model development, and figures are available in GitHub at https://github.com/mira-create/wigginton_chlorine_model.

sec.title: Systematic Review
sec.p: We identified 1,366 unique articles in the systematic review of rate constants for virus inactivation by free chlorine. Of these, 497 underwent full-text review and 72 were included in the final data set, resulting in 570 extracted chlorine disinfection rate constants. The manuscripts included in the final data set reported chlorine doses in a variety of ways, with most papers either reporting CT values (28 out of 72) or providing an initial chlorine concentration and enough information to assume that the concentration remained nearly constant throughout the experiment (34 out of 72; see SI 2 for more details). Many articles were excluded due to insufficient characterization of chlorine decay, chlorine concentrations that were higher than 20 mg Cl2/L, or a lack of infectivity measurements (Figure S1). The rate constants extracted by the two reviewers had a Lin’s concordance correlation coefficient of 0.997 (95% CI: 0.994–1.000; Figure S2) and 545 (95.6%) were within ten percent of each other for the same data point. Instances with >10% difference were due to a lack of plot resolution in the reviewed studies.

The resulting inactivation data represent 82 viruses from 18 families and five of seven recognized Baltimore classes (Figure 1a). The data contain no rate constants from Baltimore classes VI (retroviruses) or VII (hepadnaviruses). In total, 408 (72%) of the 570 extracted rate constants were for (+)ssRNA viruses, 90 (16%) for dsDNA viruses, 43 (7.9%) for dsRNA viruses, 20 (3.5%) for ssDNA viruses, and 7 (1.2%) for (−)ssRNA viruses. The most studied Baltimore classes contain human viruses that can be transmitted via water and common bacteriophage surrogates for human viruses. The specific viruses with the greatest number of reported rate constants were poliovirus 1 Mahoney (86), human adenovirus 2 (48), and MS2 bacteriophage (46).

caption.p: Inactivation rate constants for viruses by free chlorine based on (a) values obtained from the literature and measured under a broad range of conditions and (b) values estimated with the model and harmonized under reference conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 20 °C, [Cl–] < 50 mM). Error bars in b represent the 95% confidence intervals and are generally wider for viruses reported in few papers or that have high unexplained variation in published rate constants. Data are colored based on Baltimore class and sorted by the value of estimated inactivation rate constants. The letters n and p in Figure 1b represent the number of rate constants reported for each virus and the number of papers that reported those rate constants, respectively. All abbreviations are presented in Supporting Information 16, and observed and estimated rate constants are presented in SI 15 and SI 17, respectively.

sec.p: Our data set is missing notable groups of viruses and data at specific water conditions that are relevant for disinfection processes. The World Health Organization (WHO) has identified ten viruses as viruses of concern for waterborne transmission.44 Of these, astroviruses, sapoviruses, and parechovirus are absent from the data set (Table S8). The lack of data for these viruses makes it difficult to assess their susceptibility to free chlorine. Free chlorine is also used as a general disinfectant beyond water treatment, and there are few rate constants meeting the requirements of our study for human respiratory viruses such as influenza viruses (5 rate constants) or coronaviruses (0 rate constants). Likewise, viruses that disrupt agricultural and food processing operations, such as livestock viruses, plant viruses, fish viruses, and dairy phages are also largely absent. Some of these identified gaps in viruses are due to the absence of cell culture assays,45 and recent culture method advances46,47 could facilitate the collection of high-quality disinfection data for additional viruses. Dividing the Baltimore class data by pH, temperature, and chloride level highlights the uneven distribution across experimental conditions (Figure S6). Whereas the (+)ssRNA viruses have broad coverage across relevant pH, temperature, and chloride conditions, other virus classes are poorly represented at certain conditions. For example, there was no or limited data collected under high chloride conditions or for pH values above 8 for a number of Baltimore classes, and environmental temperature and pH ranges are poorly represented for the (−)ssRNA viruses.

The second-order inactivation rate constants spanned 5 orders of magnitude, from 0.0196 to 1150 L mg–1 min–1. Rate constants for individual viruses varied greatly across studies. For example, inactivation rate constants for poliovirus 1 Mahoney ranged from 0.0792 to 315 L mg–1 min–1. High variation in reported inactivation rate constants likely stems from the wide range of experimental conditions encompassed in the data, such as temperature and pH. Researchers frequently contextualize their disinfection study findings by comparing their data sets with data in the literature collected under similar conditions. However, we found that efforts to narrow down experimental conditions led to drastic decreases in the size of the data set without increasing the precision of inactivation rate constant estimates (Figure S7). For example, narrowing the data set to rate constants collected at approximately room temperature (15–25 °C) and pH values between 6 and 7 reduced the number of viruses represented in the data set from 82 to 28 and the number of rate constants decreased from 570 to 94. Surprisingly, narrowing the conditions did not decrease the variance in reported rate constants for an individual virus; for example, the rate constant standard deviations for MS2 bacteriophage and poliovirus 1 Mahoney increased from 54.5 to 69.1 L mg–1 min–1 and from 46.4 to 56.4 L mg–1 min–1, respectively. We therefore applied statistical modeling to quantify trends between viruses under a range of environmental conditions.

sec.title: Primary Model
sec.p: We selected terms for our primary model using best-subset analysis based on AIC. Model selection resulted in the following equation for a rate constant i collected in paper p(i) for virus v(i) (AIC = 584, R2 = 0.868):where yi is the log10 of the inactivation rate constant, β values are the model coefficients, ηp(i) is the random effect coefficient for the paper, and ϵi is the model residual. Terms in the primary model include an intercept, temperature, high chloride, pH, ΔpH2, and several interactions (SI 10).

sec.title: Terms Selected with Best-Subset Analysis
sec.p: The AIC best-subset model suggests that experimental conditions related to pH, chloride concentration, the interaction between pH and chloride, ΔpH2, and the interaction between temperature and pH contribute to chlorine inactivation of viruses, matching results of previous studies.9,10,12,13,48,49 Multiple terms related to [H+] were tested, and pH was selected as opposed to αHOCl (calculated using [H+] concentration). pH and αHOCl are highly colinear (Pearson correlation = 0.951), and the selection of pH over αHOCl could be due to the role OCl– plays in virus inactivation or the role that pH-sensitive functional groups in the virion play in chlorine disinfection (e.g., amino acid side chains). Water type and virus purification level were not selected in the primary model despite evidence that they affect virus disinfection with chlorine.50−52 This may be due to the classifications we used for these variables; each category encompasses experiments with a range of characteristics. More granular classification, however, risked overfitting the model due to the small numbers of reported rate constants in the resulting categories.

Of the interactions between experimental conditions (temperature, pH, ΔpH2) and virus characteristics (Baltimore class or DNA vs RNA genome), the following interactions were selected: dsRNA and pH, ssDNA and temperature, dsDNA and temperature, DNA and pH, and DNA and ΔpH2. No interactions between (−)ssRNA and experimental conditions were selected, likely due to the sparsity of (−)ssRNA rate constants in the data set. The selection of these interactions suggests that rate constants for different virus classes may be impacted differently by temperature and pH. There is little published research on how different viruses respond differently to changes in experimental conditions during chlorine disinfection. We anticipate that the selected interaction terms at the Baltimore class level were driven by interactions taking place at smaller taxonomic units such as virus family, genus, species, etc. Our current data set, however, is insufficient to test if the model is improved by including interactions between smaller taxonomic units and experimental conditions using fixed effects.

sec.title: Sensitivity Analyses
sec.p: We conducted several analyses to test the approaches and assumptions used in generating our model. The best-subset BIC model (AIC = 589, R2 = 0.867) includes all terms from the AIC model, except for ΔpH2, the interaction between temperature and pH, and the interaction between dsDNA and temperature. This suggests that there is less evidence that these terms contribute to chlorine inactivation rates than for terms included in both models. We focus our results on the AIC model because the primary purpose of the statistical model is to calibrate reported inactivation rate constants of different viruses for differences in experimental conditions and to produce efficient estimates of virus inactivation rate constants.39

In an analysis of multicollinearity, each term excluded during the best-subset analysis was added into the model, and the variance inflation factor of the subsequent model was reported (SI 6.1). αHOCl displayed a concerning aGSIF value (aGSIF > 10), indicating that it was highly multicollinear with terms already in the model. The other tested noninteraction terms (year of publication, water type, purification) do not display a high level of multicollinearity. Once the experimental terms were selected, we tested the effect of different virus taxonomic units in lieu of virus name on the AIC (SI 6.2). All tested independent taxonomic variables (i.e., Baltimore class, family, genus, and species) lowered the AIC relative to a model with no taxonomic variables, with virus name resulting in the lowest AIC.

In the maximum entropy chloride sensitivity analysis, we tested the best-subset model after switching the chloride values of the 11 points where chloride level was inferred. Most estimates at reference conditions (73/82 viruses) changed by less than 3% in magnitude, with by far the largest percent change in estimated inactivation rate constant (32.59%) occurring with Coxsackievirus B2, a virus with only one reported inactivation rate constant and where the chloride level was inferred. Coefficients for most terms that were not virus names (10/11) changed by less than 3% in magnitude (SI 7), except for the coefficient for high chloride, which decreased by 3.22%. We concluded that the model results are largely insensitive to potential assumption errors in chloride concentrations.

In the preliminary forward-selection model, the addition of a paper-specific identifier as a random effect improved the model, however the addition of corresponding author as a second random effect did not lead to further improvement. This indicates model fit is not improved by allowing rate constants from different papers written by the same corresponding author to be correlated.

sec.title: Model Estimates Under Reference Conditions
sec.p: Whereas the systematic review highlighted the limited number of viruses with rate constants collected under similar experimental conditions, the model enables harmonization of rate constants for all viruses included in the data set. We use the term harmonize to describe the process of utilizing the model to present data collected under different conditions in a consistent manner.

Under reference conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 20 °C, [Cl–] < 50 mM), the estimated inactivation rate constants for the 82 viruses identified in the systematic review spanned 3 orders of magnitude (0.555 L mg–1 min–1 to 490 L mg–1 min–1; Supporting Information 15); this is 2 orders of magnitude smaller than the range spanned by the systematic review results under all conditions (0.0196 to 1150 L mg–1 min–1). The confidence intervals generated by the model are smaller for viruses that were reported in multiple studies and under a range of experimental conditions (Figure 1b). For example, the confidence intervals for widely studied MS2 bacteriophage under reference conditions (95% CI: 7.71–18.3 L mg–1 min–1) and poliovirus 1 Mahoney under reference conditions (95% CI: 4.18–11.1 L mg–1 min–1) are relatively small. In contrast, the confidence intervals for the rate constants under reference conditions for influenza A/WhooperSwan/Mongolia/244/2005 (95% CI: 3.16–278 L mg–1 min–1) and rhesus rotavirus G3P[3] (95% CI: 0.0506–6.69 L mg–1 min–1) were notably wide, primarily because they were reported in a single paper. Large confidence intervals limited our ability to determine statistical differences between many of the viruses; for example, only 32 out of the 81 (44.0%) viruses in the model had estimated inactivation rate constants under reference conditions that differed statistically (with nominal p < 0.05) from the virus MS2 bacteriophage. Additional chlorine inactivation data for viruses that have been previously studied would help identify greater distinctions between the viruses.

Despite these large confidence intervals, the harmonized virus rate constants do exhibit trends between certain virus groupings. The (+)ssRNA viruses and dsDNA viruses are particularly important in water treatment due to the prevalence of viruses such as norovirus, coxsackievirus, and adenoviruses belonging to these classes. The harmonized data set suggests dsDNA viruses are generally more susceptible to free chlorine than the (+)ssRNA viruses (Figure 1b). The dsDNA viruses are larger than the ssRNA viruses (Table S11) and thus contain more protein targets susceptible to attack by free chlorine.28

The (+)ssRNA viruses exhibited a broad range of chlorine susceptibilities (Figure 2). The (+)ssRNA enteroviruses were the most represented virus genus in our data set and exhibited estimated rate constants that spanned nearly 2 orders of magnitude at reference conditions, from 0.555 L mg–1 min–1 (95% CI: 0.119–2.58) for CVB5 l070215 to 41.4 L mg–1 min–1 (95% CI: 9.61–178) for enterovirus 70. Several enteroviruses, including the widely studied pathogens CVB5 Faulkner and poliovirus 1 MK 500, had among the lowest rate constants at reference conditions. Differences in the exposed cysteine, methionine, and tyrosine residues within the virion capsids may drive the large rate constant diversity observed among enteroviruses.25,30 (+)ssRNA human norovirus is the leading cause of waterborne illness in the United States53 and was not represented in our data set. This is most likely because of the challenges associated with culturing this virus. Three related viruses (murine norovirus 1, murine norovirus s7-pp3, and feline calicivirus f9) had higher estimated inactivation rate constants than nearly all enterovirus estimated rate constants at reference conditions, and a number of the differences were statistically significant. These (+)ssRNA virus results suggest that, to the extent which surrogates represent human norovirus inactivation, noroviruses are effectively disinfected when chlorine treatment is designed to disinfect the most conservative human enteroviruses. Future research should expand the diversity of (+)ssRNA viruses tested with free chlorine to confirm whether the wide variation in chlorine susceptibility observed among enteroviruses is common across other genera as well.

caption.p: Estimated chlorine inactivation rate constants generated by the model under reference conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 20 °C, [Cl–] < 50 mM) for all (+)ssRNA viruses with experimental data extracted in systematic review, colored by virus genus. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals of the rate constant estimates.

sec.p: Other viruses with especially low estimated inactivation rate constants (below around 4 L mg–1 min–1 at reference conditions) include (−)ssRNA infectious hematopoietic necrosis virus (IHNV), (−)ssRNA influenza A virus (H1N1), dsRNA rhesus rotavirus G3P, and ssDNA Kilham rat virus. These types of viruses are often overlooked in systematic reviews that focus primarily on common human waterborne viruses.32,54,55 Future research should confirm their resistance to free chlorine and probe the traits that contribute to their resistance.

sec.title: Model Quantifies Role of Experimental Conditions
sec.p: Beyond harmonizing the data, the model quantifies the impact of pH, temperature, and chloride conditions on disinfection rate constants. The temperature, pH, and chloride trends generated by the model agreed with findings from previous studies focused on individual viruses. Specifically, the modeled inactivation rate constants increase with increasing temperature, decrease with increasing pH, and increase with increasing chloride levels (Figure 3). The extent that changing pH or temperature impacts estimated virus inactivation rate constants varies between the Baltimore classes. For the (+)ssRNA MS2 bacteriophage under reference conditions, a temperature increase from 20 to 25 °C at low chloride conditions and a pH of 7.53 increases the estimated inactivation rate from 11.9 L mg–1 min–1 (95% CI: 7.71–18.3 L mg–1 min–1) to 15.3 L mg–1 min–1 (95% CI: 9.79–23.8 L mg–1 min–1), or a or a 28.6% increase (95% CI: 17.5%–46.4%). A 1 unit increase in pH from 7.53 to 8.53 under reference conditions decreases the inactivation rate constant from 11.9 L mg–1 min–1 (95% CI: 7.71–18.3 L mg–1 min–1) to 7.46 L mg–1 min–1 (95% CI: 4.82–11.6 L mg–1 min–1), or a 37.1% decrease (95% CI:24.4%–56.5%).

caption.p: Harmonized virus inactivation rate constants (L mg–1 min–1) and the associate confidence intervals generated by the model under different experimental conditions for viruses with five or more data points in the data set. Inactivation rate constants are arranged from lowest to highest in each figure. Note that x axes scales differ in these plots. (a) Inactivation rate constants at reference conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 20 °C, [Cl–] < 50 mM). (b) Inactivation rate constants at elevated pH conditions (pH = 10, T = 20 °C, [Cl–] < 50 mM). (c) Inactivation rate constants at low temperature conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 5 °C, [Cl–] < 50 mM). (d) Inactivation rate constants at elevated chloride conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 20 °C, [Cl–] > 50 mM).

sec.p: As noted above, the model incorporates Baltimore class-specific interactions such as dsRNA with pH and ssDNA with temperature. As a result, changing experimental conditions affects the estimated rate constants of viruses from distinct Baltimore classes in varying ways. For example, when the pH decreases from 10 to 7.53, the estimated rate constant increases by 4.03-fold (95% CI:3.00–5.40) for (+)ssRNA viruses, by 1.27-fold fold (95% CI:0.70–2.29) for dsRNA viruses, and by 61.9-fold (95% CI:32.80–116.84) for dsDNA viruses. This suggests that the relative chlorine susceptibility of viruses in different classes depends on experimental conditions. Indeed, echovirus 1 Farouk is estimated to be more resistant than human adenovirus 5 under reference conditions (pH = 7.53, T = 20 °C, [Cl-] < 50 mM), but human adenovirus 5 is estimated to be more resistant than echovirus 1 Farouk at high pH (pH = 10, T = 20 °C, [Cl-] < 50 mM). These results have implications for the selection of virus surrogates for free chlorine, as a surrogate may be more conservative than a human virus under some conditions and more susceptible under others. Future research should investigate the mechanistic reasons behind these virus-specific effects of experimental conditions.

Results from the model provide an opportunity to revisit U.S. EPA requirements for chlorine disinfection of viruses. These rules were developed based on hepatitis A virus (HAV) HM175 data from a 1988 study,20 and incorporated a safety factor of 3× the CT values.17,56 At the time of the 1988 study, the measured HAV rate constants were relatively conservative compared to data published on other viruses. Our harmonized data, however, suggest that HAV inactivation rate constants are in the midrange of rate constants for human pathogens, with 44 of the 81 other viruses estimated to be more resistant to free chlorine inactivation than HAV HM175 under reference conditions. The U.S. EPA requires the same CT values for pH values between 6 and pH 9 based on results from the 1988 study. We converted the required CT values for 4-log10 virus reduction to equivalent rate constants, allowing us to directly compare the U.S. EPA requirements with our model results (see Supporting Information 13 for calculation). Our model suggests that pH changes from 6 to 9 do in fact affect chlorine inactivation rate constants for most viruses. For example, the MS2 bacteriophage rate constant increases by 3.24-fold (95% CI: 2.50–4.23) between pH 6 and pH 9 under reference temperature and chloride conditions. With respect to temperature, the U.S. EPA CT requirements decrease by 6-fold between 0.5 and 25 °C; the statistical model, however, estimates only a 3.42-fold decrease (95% CI: 2.57–4.56) for the MS2 bacteriophage rate constant under reference pH and chloride conditions.

The model results suggest that current CT requirements may not achieve 4-log10 inactivation for many viruses, especially under elevated temperatures and pH values. For every condition described in the U.S. EPA requirements, our model estimates that at least five of the viruses in our data set do not achieve 4-log10 inactivation based on the required CT values (Table 1). Many enteroviruses did not achieve disinfection requirements under a range of conditions; CVB5 Faulkner, for example, only achieves 4-log10 inactivation at pH 6 and 5 °C, and PV1MK500 achieves the requirements under approximately half of the relevant conditions (SI 13.2; SI 18). On the other hand, HAV H175, the virus originally used to develop the U.S. EPA requirements, and MS2 bacteriophage, a common (+)ssRNA surrogate, are both estimated to achieve 4-log10 inactivation under all the conditions listed in the CT tables. Combined, the model results confirm a concern raised in the most recent U.S. EPA Six-Year Review that current regulations may be insufficiently conservative to achieve CVB5 removal.17

caption.title: Number and Percentage of Viruses (out of 82 total viruses) Estimated by Our Model Not to Meet the Requirement for 4-log10 Removal under U.S. EPA Required CT Values

tr.th: Number of viruses not meeting requirement
Number of viruses not meeting requirement
Number of viruses not meeting requirement
Number of viruses not meeting requirement
Number of viruses not meeting requirement

sec.p: We should note that the rate constants used in developing the model represented only the first-order portion of inactivation curves. To compare our results with the U.S. EPA requirements for 4-log10 inactivation, we assumed that the estimated inactivation rate constants could be applied to 4-log10 inactivation, although approximately 40% of the studies in our data set exhibited evidence of tailing before 4-log10 inactivation. This means that even more viruses than presented in Table 1 would fall short of 4-log10 inactivation with CT values in the U.S. EPA requirements.

sec.title: Correlation of Error within Papers
sec.p: Random effects (i.e., the correlated within-paper error) played a large role in the estimated chlorine inactivation rate constants, indicating that rate constants reported in the same paper tend to be systematically higher or lower than values reported in other papers even after accounting for differences in experimental conditions. Concretely, the standard deviation of the random effects was 0.423 log10 units (Figure S10), meaning that a hypothetical paper drawn from the population has a 32% chance of reporting rate constants biased by at least ±2.6 fold (= 100.423). Note that 32% represents the probability that a random variable drawn from a Gaussian distribution is more than 1 standard deviation above or below the mean. The adjusted Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC), which refers to the correlation between rate constants calculated from the same paper, is 0.703 (95% CI: 0.539-0.793). Based on this, 70.3% of variance unexplained by the selected experimental conditions is explained by the random paper effects.

An estimated random effect of zero, observed in 8 papers, indicates that these papers exclusively reported inactivation data for viruses not reported in any other publications. In these cases, the paper random effect is perfectly collinear with the virus name. A small, nonzero random effect suggests that the paper investigated viruses that had been previously studied under commonly studied conditions and yielded results close to those of other papers. A large random effect indicates that the paper studied viruses that had been studied elsewhere at commonly studied conditions and produced results largely different to those produced by other papers. Papers exhibiting large random effects may be systematically biased, possibly because certain experimental details that influence chlorine inactivation were not precisely reported (e.g., room temperature may refer to a variety of temperatures) or because of the difficulty in categorizing some variables (e.g., buffer composition). Any driver of chlorine inactivation not described by a model term but that is correlated within a paper would contribute to the random effect of that paper. The model does not include terms reflecting every possible driver of chlorine inactivation due to our inability to extract such variables from each paper. For example, bromide concentration and host cell type were experimental features that likely affect the rate constants but were not represented in the model.38,57

sec.title: Environmental Implications
sec.p: This comprehensive systematic review of chlorine inactivation of viruses represents an important resource for scientists and practitioners. Previous systematic reviews of chlorine inactivation of viruses focused on a narrow range of water conditions55 or on virus families commonly emphasized in water treatment, including enteroviruses, adenoviruses, and caliciviruses.32,54,55 By reviewing chlorine inactivation across all studied viruses and conditions, we were able to build a statistical model that filled in important experimental condition gaps and harmonized data for all viruses under similar environmental conditions. We have produced a Web site for researchers to use our model (https://mira-chaplin.shinyapps.io/wigginton_chlorine_model/). This allows for immediate estimations of inactivation rate constants for all studied viruses over the range of conditions encompassed in the original data set (pH 4–10, temperature 0–35 °C).

The data set and model help identify human viruses that are most resistant to free chlorine and thus assess when current disinfection practice adequately inactivates the most resistant waterborne viruses. Beyond viruses known to cause human disease, in general, and undergo waterborne transmission, in particular, there is value in having a data set that includes the broadest diversity of viruses possible, including animal viruses and bacteriophages. A comprehensive data set on chlorine inactivation of viruses helps identify the physical characteristics that render viruses more resistant to disinfectants. This in turn prepares scientists and practitioners to predict the susceptibility of an emerging virus to free chlorine treatment, regardless of how closely the emerging virus resembles viruses previously associated with disease or waterborne transmission. We therefore encourage the continued collection of high-quality free chlorine disinfection data for viruses not represented in our data set.

To provide external validity and thus measure random effects, future studies of virus reactions with chlorine should include controls with reference water treatment conditions (i.e., room temperature, neutral pH, low chloride concentrations) and include a widely studied reference virus for benchmarking against other virus disinfection data. Potential reference viruses include MS2 bacteriophage, CVB5 Faulkner, human adenovirus 2, echovirus 1 Farouk, and echovirus 11 Gregory, as these have all been studied in at least five papers and under a variety of conditions. Future studies should also report detailed experimental conditions, especially those known to impact chlorine inactivation of viruses. At a minimum, these should include temperature, pH, chloride concentration, buffer chemical composition and constituent concentrations, and procedures taken to remove chlorine demand and purify viruses.

The statistical model is a valuable resource for selecting surrogates for human waterborne viruses. Process surrogates are commonly used to stand in for all human viruses to test water treatment processes with site-specific reactors and waters. Likewise, experimental surrogates are used to estimate chlorine inactivation of specific human viruses that are difficult to culture (e.g., murine norovirus used in lieu of human norovirus58). For chlorine treatment, surrogates should be equally or more resistant to chlorine than the human viruses of interest. MS2 bacteriophage is the most common surrogate because it is structurally similar to a number of important human enteric viruses, relatively easy to culture, and safe to work with. Our results suggest that although MS2 bacteriophage has one of the lowest bacteriophage inactivation rate constants at reference conditions, it is not a conservative surrogate for many human viruses with free chlorine. It is an especially poor surrogate for enteroviruses, with 40 out of 43 studied enteroviruses having estimated rate constants lower than MS2 bacteriophage under all environmental conditions. CVB5 Faulkner had the fifth-lowest estimated rate constant of all viruses included in the systematic review, and the lowest estimated rate constant of all viruses that represented at least five data points. Whereas CVB5 could serve as a conservative surrogate for human viruses with free chlorine due to its especially high chlorine resistance, its BSL2 characterization means it is not easily studied in all laboratories. Future studies should identify a bacteriophage or animal virus that is at least as conservative as CVB5 under a wide range of physicochemical conditions.

Our model reveals that current regulations may not achieve adequate removal of several important waterborne viruses, including many coxsackieviruses. Disease severity, in addition to chlorine resistance, is a consideration in the design of virus disinfection regulations, with HAV initially selected as a reference virus because it causes more severe health effects than CVB5.17 Our model could be combined with expected initial concentrations for a variety of human viruses and a risk assessment to select a conservative reference virus for disinfection regulations and update CT tables based on risk and disinfection susceptibility.

There are limitations to the literature review and statistical modeling. First, the data set used to develop the model encompassed a pH range between 4 and 10 and temperature values between 0 and 35 °C. We do not recommend applying the model outside of these ranges. The model was built with rate constants calculated from the first-order portion of inactivation curves and tailing was frequently observed after the first two to four logs of inactivation. Care should therefore be taken when applying these models beyond 2–4 log10 inactivation. We anticipate that our data set and model will facilitate further research on the sources and prediction of tailing during free chlorine disinfection. Our model does not incorporate host cells used for enumerating viruses despite a recent study demonstrating their effect on chlorine inactivation of coxsackieviruses.57 Further modeling targeted at well-studied viruses with similar host receptors is necessary to estimate the effect of host cell types on inactivation rates. Finally, some of the model assumptions are biased by the limited data set, including the assumption that viruses in the same Baltimore class respond similarly to changes in environmental conditions. As more data becomes available, the model can be updated to make finer distinctions. Ultimately, this literature review and statistical analysis provide tools for researchers and practitioners to identify new research directions and improve treatment processes.

notes.title: Supporting Information Available
notes.p:
The Supporting Information is available free of charge at https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.est.4c02885.
SI 1: Supplementary methods for systematic review and data extraction. SI 2: Details on calculation of inactivation rate constants. SI 3: Variable description and derivation. SI 4: Variable and model residual distributions. SI 5: Derivation of interaction terms. SI 6: Model development sensitivity analyses. SI 7: Chloride sensitivity analysis. SI 8: Examination of limitations in the data set. SI 9: Data comparisons under narrowed environmental conditions. S10: Model coefficients. SI 11: Variables selected in alternate models. SI 12: Genome length and diameter. SI 13: Assessment of U.S. EPA standards. SI 14: Random effects. (PDF)
SI 15: Data set from systematic review (XLSX)
SI 16: Virus name abbreviations (XLSX)
SI 17: Estimated inactivation rate constants of all viruses under reference conditions (XLSX)
SI 18: Viruses estimated not to attain 4-log10 inactivation at U.S. EPA required CT value (XLSX)
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: This work was supported by National Science Foundation project #2015187. M.C. was supported by a National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship (award no. #2241144). K.L. was supported by the Jack Kent Cooke Foundation and the Summer Undergraduate Research in Engineering Program.
